summary: On-call pain is not an inevitable cost of owning code in production — it's a sociotechnical problem requiring both technical fixes (alert discipline, SLOs, proper tooling) and organizational solutions (management accountability, fair rotations, humane policies). Engineering owns their code, but management owns the responsibility to make on-call sustainable, and failure to do so is a five-alarm fire that should be fixed with urgency.
key_points: On-call rotations are sociotechnical systems requiring both people and technical solutions
Engineering owns code in production; management owns making that sustainable
Alert discipline requires only two lanes: 'wake me up' and 'deal with this later'
The ideal on-call rotation has 7-8 people for sustainability
On-call problems often mask deeper organizational dysfunction
money_quotes: It is engineering's job to own their code in production. It is management's job to make sure it doesn't suck.

If management isn't holding up their end, if they don't allocate enough time to fix the underlying problems – if they run a feature factory that never stops to refactor or invest in reliability work – then on-call will never get better, and you should leave.
I would never ask a new parent to be on call. Being woken up by ONE instrument of chaos is all anyone should ever have to cope with at any given time.
Pay them so goddamn well the finance team starts squawking about the need to pay down that reliability debt.

Engineers should never have to quietly suffer the pain of flaky software and unhappy users alone. Give management pain too until they take their jobs seriously enough to see that reliability issues get fixed.
key_insight: On-call suffering is never just a technical problem — it's a management accountability problem, and the two-way pact between engineering ownership and management support must be enforced for on-call to be sustainable.
